items.reality: Types are erased during compilation; the running JavaScript has no knowledge of them, so runtime validation is still your responsibility.
items.example: Compile `function f(x: User) { ... }` and inspect the JS: it's just `function f(x) { ... }`. The `User` constraint is gone. Validate at the boundary.

items.myth: interface and type are interchangeable in all cases.
items.reality: interface is for object shapes (and supports extension/merging); type also expresses unions, intersections, tuples and primitive aliases that interface cannot.
items.example: `type Result = Success | Failure` (union) — `interface` can't do this. `interface Shape { ... }` followed by another `interface Shape { ... }` merges them — `type` can't do this. Use each where it fits.

items.myth: TypeScript matches types by name (nominal typing).
items.reality: It's structural: any value with the required shape is accepted, regardless of the declared type's name.
